Title: Comparison of Stepped Care Delivery Against a Single, Empirically Validated Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy Program for Youth With Anxiety: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
Authors: R. Rapee, Heidi J. Lyneham, V. Wuthrich, M. Chatterton, J. Hudson, M. Kangas, C. Mihalopoulos
Effect Size: No significant outcome difference; stepped care used 774 vs 897 therapist minutes.
Key Finding: Stepped care matched standard CBT on outcomes while saving therapist time.
Limitation: Tests treatment delivery sequencing, not a universal→targeted→specialist system.
Summary: This randomized clinical trial compared stepped care with a single empirically validated CBT program in 281 anxious youth. The two approaches did not differ significantly on outcomes, while stepped care used less therapist time.

Title: Psychological stepped care for anxious adolescents in community mental health services: A pilot effectiveness trial
Authors: V. Wuthrich, R. Rapee, Lauren F. McLellan, A. Wignall, T. Jagiello, Melissa M. Norberg, Jessica Belcher
Effect Size: Similar clinical benefit; less therapy time; no abstract-level numeric effect estimate.
Key Finding: Stepped care looked workable in routine services.
Limitation: Small pilot; treatment-as-usual comparator; not an end-to-end system trial.
Summary: A pilot RCT in community mental health services found stepped care used less therapy time with similar clinical benefits and quality-adjusted life years versus treatment as usual. The abstract frames stepped care as promising, but the study was small and did not test a whole public-health system.

Title: Stepped-care versus therapist-guided, internet-based cognitive behaviour therapy for childhood and adolescent anxiety: A non-inferiority trial
Authors: Sonja March, Susan H Spence, Larry Myers, Martelle Ford, Genevieve E Smith, C. Donovan
Effect Size: Noninferior at 12 weeks and 9 months; 77% of completers in both arms were free of the primary anxiety diagnosis by 9 months.
Key Finding: Stepped-care ICBT performed about as well as therapist-guided ICBT.
Limitation: Only a delivery-sequencing trial, not a full system trial.
Summary: This preregistered noninferiority trial found stepped-care internet CBT was noninferior to therapist-guided internet CBT at 12 weeks and 9 months. It supports one stepped-care delivery variant, but not a complete integrated model spanning universal, targeted, and specialist tiers.

Title: Stepped Care Versus Stratified Care for Youth with Anxiety Disorders: a Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial.
Authors: E. Olsson, P. Andrén, E. Claesdotter-Knutsson, Kajsa Dellgran, O. Castro, A. Möttus, D. Mataix-Cols, Sabina Gušić, Sean Perrin, E. Serlachius, M. Cervin
Effect Size: Responder rates 40% vs 75% at the primary endpoint; no efficacy comparison was performed.
Key Finding: A definitive stepped-care versus stratified-care trial appears feasible.
Limitation: Feasibility-only; not powered for efficacy and not an integrated system test.
Summary: In this pilot randomized trial, stepped care was compared with stratified care for youth anxiety disorders. The study found the trial was feasible, but it was explicitly not powered to compare efficacy.

Title: The Efficacy and Cost-Effectiveness of Stepped Care Prevention and Treatment for Depressive and/or Anxiety Disorders: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
Authors: F. Ho, W. Yeung, Tommy H. Ng, C. Chan
Effect Size: 10 RCTs; stepped-care treatment better than care as usual for anxiety symptoms.
Key Finding: Stepped-care interventions appear helpful for anxiety treatment.
Limitation: Focused on self-help-containing trials; not a full integrated system evaluation.
Summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis included 10 randomized controlled trials of stepped-care prevention and treatment for depressive and/or anxiety disorders. It found stepped-care treatment outperformed care as usual for anxiety symptoms, but it still stops short of evaluating a complete universal→targeted→specialist care system in youth anxiety.
